front: In array-based queue implementation, what do the 'front' and 'rear' pointers/indexes represent?
back: The 'front' pointer/index indicates the position of the next element to be dequeued (oldest element). The 'rear' pointer/index indicates the position where the next element will be enqueued.

front: What is the common initial setup for 'front' and 'rear' pointers in an empty queue implemented with an array?
back: Both 'front' and 'rear' are typically initialized to -1 to signify that the queue is empty.

front: What is the main drawback of a simple linear array implementation of a queue?
back: It can lead to "false overflow" where the rear pointer reaches the end of the array, but empty spaces exist at the front because of previous dequeues. This is solved using a Circular Queue.

front: What are the time complexities for the basic queue operations (enqueue and dequeue) when implemented with an array?
back: Both enqueue (adding an element) and dequeue (removing an element) are O(1) constant time operations, as they only involve updating pointers and accessing an array index.

front: What condition is checked to determine if a linear queue (implemented with an array) is full?
back: The queue is considered full if the 'rear' pointer has reached the last index of the array (i.e., rear == size_of_array - 1).
